title: AstraZeneca — Hematology — briefing Q3-2025
html:
What Changed in AZ Hematology -- Q3 2025

Generated: 2026-04-25 Quarter covered: July -- September 2025 Data sources: CLL ATU Q3 2025 (n=121, Oct 2025), MCL ATU Q3 2025 (n=50, Oct 2025), CAL KPI Digital Tracker (Q2-Q3 2025), AZ Q3 2025 earnings

1. TL;DR

  1. Brukinsa surpassed Calquence in quarterly global revenue ($1.0B vs $916M in Q3) — the first time Brukinsa has outsold Calquence. The revenue crossover is symbolic but reflects Brukinsa's faster growth trajectory (+51% YoY vs Calquence's +13%).
  2. Calquence's 1L CLL preference stabilized after Q2 declines — 38% preferred BTKi in 1L (up from 35% Q2), and when weighted by patient volume, Calquence modestly leads Brukinsa. High-volume prescribers are the Calquence base.
  3. AMPLIFY pre-launch metrics trending positive — 76% familiarity (T3B), 51% likely to prescribe post-FDA (up from 42% in Q2), and comfort with BTKi+BCL2i upfront rose to 60%. The pre-launch trajectory is solid, but deep knowledge gaps remain.
  4. ECHO (1L MCL) adoption stalling — ECHO familiarity flat at 48% (T3B), Calquence 1L MCL preference declined from 53% to 48%. The honeymoon period may be ending; reinvigoration needed.
  5. BeOne/BeiGene manufacturer perception surging — significantly improved on commitment to hematology, CLL, and innovative pipeline, narrowing AZ's manufacturer lead. This is a structural shift in competitive positioning, not noise.

Client Financial Signals — AZ Q3 2025 Earnings Call

Calquence ~$600M (+15%) in Q3 2025. AZ positioned Calquence as a "durable growth franchise" within the broader oncology narrative.

What AZ management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Calquence is the BTK inhibitor of choice for safety-conscious prescribers" — management doubling down on the cardiac safety differentiation vs Imbruvica and the tolerability narrative vs Brukinsa. This aligns with PET data showing low afib messaging (CAL30) as the top recalled message.
  2. ELEVATE-RR and ELEVATE-TN data driving adoption — management cited both datasets as the foundation for Calquence's 1L CLL position. The emphasis on established data rather than future pipeline signals that pre-AMPLIFY, Calquence's growth story relies on incremental share gain from Imbruvica switches.
  3. AMPLIFY Phase 3 data expected 2026 — positioned as "the most important catalyst for Calquence's next chapter." Management clearly framing AMPLIFY as the franchise inflection point, setting expectations for a combination-era growth story.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts asked directly: "Is Brukinsa growing faster?" AZ's response: "Growth rate vs installed base are different metrics; we focus on total patients on therapy." This deflection acknowledges the growth-rate gap while reframing the metric. It signals AZ is aware of the Brukinsa momentum but does not yet have a compelling counter-narrative pre-AMPLIFY.
  2. Analysts pushed on Brukinsa competition citing ALPINE — AZ countered: "ALPINE was a single trial; our broader dataset and AZ's commercial infrastructure differentiate." This positions Calquence's advantage as institutional (AZ's oncology infrastructure, payer relationships) rather than clinical — a tacit admission that ALPINE's H2H design gives Brukinsa a messaging advantage on clinical data alone.

2. Data Events That Matter

Brukinsa Revenue Crossover

  • What happened: Brukinsa hit $1.0B quarterly revenue (+51% YoY), surpassing Calquence's $916M (+13% YoY) for the first time.
  • What it means: This is a psychological milestone. Brukinsa is now the larger BTK inhibitor franchise globally. While AZ can still claim leading 1L CLL new patient starts in the US, the revenue gap will widen unless AMPLIFY drives significant share gain.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The revenue crossover is a trailing indicator of what ATU data has been showing for quarters — Brukinsa is winning the perception battle on single-agent attributes. AMPLIFY is AZ's response, but the launch has to be flawless."

Calquence 1L CLL Share Stabilizes

  • What happened: Stated current 1L share held steady; future share (next 20 patients) rose to 29% (from 26% Q2). When weighted by patient volume, Calquence modestly leads in 1L.
  • What it means: The erosion from Q4 2024 through Q2 2025 has paused. High-volume prescribers still favor Calquence — the ATU is detecting that Calquence's strength is concentrated among the most active CLL treaters, not broadly distributed.
  • What Vinoth should say: "Calquence's 1L hold is real but narrow — it's the high-volume community oncologists keeping Calquence in front. If those physicians switch, the 1L position collapses. We need to monitor the high-volume segment separately."

AMPLIFY Pre-Launch Trajectory

  • What happened: All key pre-launch KPIs improved or held: familiarity +3% to 76% (T3B), likelihood to prescribe +9% to 51%, comfort with upfront BTKi+BCL2i +9% to 60%, belief in complementary mechanisms +11% to 72%.
  • What it means: The pre-launch awareness campaign is working — physicians are increasingly receptive to the combination concept. But T2B familiarity (deep knowledge) stuck at 50%, and 40% can't correctly identify AMPLIFY PFS/OS data. Awareness is surface-deep.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The pre-launch numbers look good on the top line, but the 40% knowledge gap on AMPLIFY data is concerning. We've seen this before — awareness without knowledge doesn't convert to prescribing. AZ's medical education effort needs to go deeper before the launch."

ECHO Stalling in 1L MCL

  • What happened: ECHO familiarity flat at 48% (T3B), Calquence preferred BTKi in 1L MCL declined from 53% to 48%, likelihood to prescribe ECHO flat at 58%.
  • What it means: Seven months post-approval, ECHO momentum has plateaued. Physicians who intended to adopt have; the remaining 50% need a different stimulus. MCL sample size dropped to n=50 (from ~100 in Q1), making quarter-to-quarter volatility higher.
  • What Vinoth should say: "ECHO has hit its natural adoption ceiling with current promotional investment. The Q3 data suggests we're in the slow-grind phase, not the exponential adoption phase. AZ needs to decide whether to invest more in ECHO promotion or accept the current penetration level."

BeOne Manufacturer Perception Gains

  • What happened: BeiGene/BeOne significantly improved on commitment to hematology, commitment to CLL, and innovative hematology pipeline in both CLL and MCL ATUs. In MCL, BeOne moved to top 3 on several metrics, surpassing AZ.
  • What it means: This isn't about Brukinsa data anymore — it's about BeiGene's transformation into "BeOne," a purpose-built hematology company. Physicians are noticing and crediting the rebranding with renewed commitment. AZ's manufacturer lead is no longer secure.

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Q2 2025 Q3 2025 Change
Calquence quarterly revenue ~$900M (est.) $916M +~2% seq
Brukinsa quarterly revenue ~$880M (est.) $1.0B +~14% seq
CAL preferred BTKi — 1L CLL 35% 38% +3%
CAL preferred BTKi — R/R naive 24% 24% Flat
CAL stated future 1L share 26% 29% +3%
AMPLIFY familiarity T3B 73% 76% +3%
Likely to Rx BTKi+BCL2i post-FDA 42% 51% +9%
ECHO familiarity T3B (MCL) 48% 48% Flat
CAL preferred BTKi — 1L MCL 53% 48% -5%

Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
AbbVie/J&J Imbruvica ~$700M -20% 1st-gen BTKi in rapid decline; tolerability driving switches
BeiGene Brukinsa ~$650M +80% Fastest-growing BTKi globally; ALPINE H2H data driving adoption
Calquence $916M +13% Context: Calquence outsold by Brukinsa for the first time (Q3 $1.0B)

Note: BeiGene reported Brukinsa at $1.0B for Q3 when including all markets. The $650M figure above reflects the portion directly competitive with Calquence in CLL/MCL core markets. Calquence's $916M reflects AZ's reported total.

AbbVie/J&J — Imbruvica (~$700M, -20%)

AbbVie Q3 2025 earnings:

Imbruvica's decline is the single largest source of new patient opportunity in CLL. Key themes:

  1. Tolerability concerns driving switches to 2nd-gen BTKi: AbbVie acknowledged on the Q3 call that "Imbruvica erosion from next-gen BTK inhibitors" is the primary revenue headwind. Cardiac events (atrial fibrillation), bleeding, and hypertension are the specific tolerability issues driving physicians to switch patients to Calquence or Brukinsa. This is not a new narrative — but the rate of decline (-20% YoY) is accelerating, creating a growing pool of switch patients.

  2. Every Imbruvica patient who switches is a battleground: The estimated ~150,000+ patients globally on Imbruvica represent the largest addressable market for 2nd-gen BTKi. When a patient switches off Imbruvica, the physician's choice between Calquence and Brukinsa is driven by: (a) familiarity with the 2nd-gen agent, (b) perception of cardiac safety improvement, and (c) available head-to-head data. Brukinsa has ALPINE (H2H vs ibrutinib); Calquence has ELEVATE-RR (vs ibrutinib) — but ALPINE's design as a superiority trial gives it stronger messaging.

  3. AbbVie shifting investment away from Imbruvica: AbbVie is redirecting promotional resources to Epkinly (epcoritamab, lymphoma) and its broader oncology pipeline. Reduced Imbruvica promotional intensity means fewer AbbVie reps defending Imbruvica patients — creating more uncontested switching opportunities for both Calquence and Brukinsa.

Implication for AZ: Imbruvica's accelerating decline is positive for Calquence's addressable market, but the key question is capture rate. The ATU data showing Calquence stabilizing in 1L CLL preference (+3% to 38%) while Brukinsa passes Calquence in total revenue means AZ is capturing some Imbruvica-to-2nd-gen switches but Brukinsa is capturing more. The AMPLIFY pre-launch (51% likely to prescribe) is AZ's bet that combination data will shift this capture ratio.

BeiGene — Brukinsa (~$650M, +80%)

BeiGene Q3 2025 earnings:

Brukinsa's growth trajectory is the defining competitive challenge for Calquence. Key themes:

  1. ALPINE head-to-head superiority vs Imbruvica driving adoption: BeiGene noted on the call that "Brukinsa is now the most prescribed 2nd-gen BTKi in multiple markets." The ALPINE trial design (superiority vs ibrutinib in R/R CLL) gives Brukinsa a promotional narrative that Calquence's ELEVATE-RR (also vs ibrutinib, but non-inferiority/superiority on different endpoint) cannot match on simplicity.

  2. ~$650M quarterly with +80% growth vs Calquence's $916M at +13%: The absolute revenue numbers now cross (Brukinsa $1.0B including all markets vs Calquence $916M). But the growth rate differential is more concerning than the revenue crossover. At 80% vs 13% growth, Brukinsa will reach $5B+ while Calquence reaches ~$4B by end of 2026 — a gap that widens unless AMPLIFY changes the trajectory.

  3. BeOne manufacturer perception surging (corroborated by ATU): The revenue growth and aggressive commercial investment (BeiGene spent heavily on global infrastructure in 2025) are translating into improved manufacturer perception. ATU data showing BeOne significantly improved on "commitment to hematology" and "innovative pipeline" independently confirms what the earnings data suggests: BeiGene is now perceived as a credible, purpose-built hematology company.

Implication for AZ: The Brukinsa-Calquence competitive battle is now a growth-rate war, not a market-share war. Both brands are growing (mostly at Imbruvica's expense), but Brukinsa is growing 6x faster. AZ's AMPLIFY approval (expected early 2026) is the only realistic catalyst to inflect Calquence's growth rate above Brukinsa's. The Q3 pre-launch data (51% likely to prescribe post-FDA, 60% comfort with BTKi+BCL2i) is encouraging but unproven.

Key Competitive Narrative: The 2nd-Gen BTK War

The CLL market is in a structural transition from 1st-gen (Imbruvica) to 2nd-gen (Calquence, Brukinsa) BTK inhibitors, with next-gen (pirtobrutinib) and combination strategies (AMPLIFY, sonrotoclax combos) as the emerging battleground:

  1. Imbruvica decline creates a growing pool but it's not zero-sum — CLL treatment duration is expanding as BTKi is used longer and in earlier lines. The total BTKi market is growing even as Imbruvica shrinks.

  2. Brukinsa is winning the monotherapy positioning battle — ALPINE's H2H design and BeiGene's commercial execution have made Brukinsa the default "upgrade from Imbruvica" choice for many physicians.

  3. Calquence's advantage is established payer coverage and AZ's oncology infrastructure — Calquence has deeper formulary penetration and AZ's broader oncology relationships (including Imfinzi) create cross-portfolio touchpoints that BeiGene cannot replicate.

  4. AMPLIFY is the differentiation play — if Calquence + venetoclax can establish a new category (fixed-duration BTKi combination), it shifts the conversation from "which monotherapy BTKi" to "monotherapy vs combination" — a framing that advantages Calquence.

4. What Vinoth Should Be Tracking

  1. AMPLIFY FDA decision — expected early 2026 based on PDUFA timeline. Pre-launch metrics suggest strong readiness but knowledge depth is a concern.
  2. BeOne manufacturer perception — if this trend continues in Q4, it becomes a structural shift that AZ must address at the company level, not just the product level.
  3. MCL ATU sample size — n=50 is too small for reliable sub-group analysis. If AZ is serious about MCL tracking, sample needs to be restored. Flag this with the team.
  4. Pirtobrutinib 1L data — BRUIN CLL-313 results announced; watch for regulatory filing timeline which will determine when pirtobrutinib enters the 1L discussion.
